coredata.dc:title: The phosphorescence property of carbon dots presenting as powder, embedded in filter paper and dispersed in solid solution
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiaobo
Zhao, Jianru
Wang, Xiaoyu
Pan, Wei
Yu, Guifeng
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has distinctive advantages over the fluorescence counterparts, such as larger Stokes shifts and longer lifetimes. It is challenging to design and prepare RTP carbon dots (CDs) because many of them need appropriate solid matrix or introducing heavy atoms for promoting intersystem crossing and suppressing vibrational dissipation between singlet and triplet states. In this paper, long-lived RTP carbon dots (EDA-CDs) were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of ethylenediamine (EDA) in phosphoric acid solution. Interestingly, at room temperature, bright phosphorescence was observed for EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder, and could last for 7 s to naked eyes, with the phosphorescence lifetime being up to 693.67 and 887.55 ms, respectively. At low temperature of 123 K, the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs in solid solution can reach the highest value of 1538 ms, while the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs presenting as solid powder is 1287.54 ms. It can be deduced that robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks formed by highly ordered hydration water molecules around EDA-CDs in solid solution at low temperature are beneficial for rigidifying the surface groups of EDA-CDs and thus the longer phosphorescence lifetime. Finally, RTP EDA-CDs had shown a great potential to be as advanced security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Long-lived phosphorescence lifetime
Anti-counting security

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of an interference fluorescent CA-CDs and inks
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China
Department of Science and Technology of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image, b) size distribution, c) the XRD profile and d) FT-IR spectrum of EDA-CDs.

a) The fluorescence spectra of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, insert: the images of EDA-CDs solution under visible and ultraviolet light. b) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution. c) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in filter paper and presenting as EDA-CDs powder, respectively. d) The phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra of the EDA-CDs presenting as powder and embedded in filter paper.

a) The fluorescence and b) phosphorescence lifetimes of EDA-CDs powder at various temperatures. c) The fluorescence intensity of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in paper and presenting as powder, and d) the phosphorescence intensity of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder under continuous excitation at 340 nm within 10 min.
Encryption/decoding tests using EDA-CDs as security ink, a) a microarray data storage pattern according to the binary codes, b) Chinese telegraph code and c) word anti-counterfeiting.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of EDA-CDs and the performance of solid phosphorescence emission.
Schematic illustration of the possible luminescence mechanism of EDA-CDs.

head.ce:title: The phosphorescence property of carbon dots presenting as powder, embedded in filter paper and dispersed in solid solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Province Key Laboratory of Applied Mycology, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Province Key Laboratory of Applied Mycology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Province Key Laboratory of Applied Mycology, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has distinctive advantages over the fluorescence counterparts, such as larger Stokes shifts and longer lifetimes. It is challenging to design and prepare RTP carbon dots (CDs) because many of them need appropriate solid matrix or introducing heavy atoms for promoting intersystem crossing and suppressing vibrational dissipation between singlet and triplet states. In this paper, long-lived RTP carbon dots (EDA-CDs) were synthesized by microwave-assisted pyrolysis of ethylenediamine (EDA) in phosphoric acid solution. Interestingly, at room temperature, bright phosphorescence was observed for EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder, and could last for 7 s to naked eyes, with the phosphorescence lifetime being up to 693.67 and 887.55 ms, respectively. At low temperature of 123 K, the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs in solid solution can reach the highest value of 1538 ms, while the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs presenting as solid powder is 1287.54 ms. It can be deduced that robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks formed by highly ordered hydration water molecules around EDA-CDs in solid solution at low temperature are beneficial for rigidifying the surface groups of EDA-CDs and thus the longer phosphorescence lifetime. Finally, RTP EDA-CDs had shown a great potential to be as advanced security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

The phosphorescence property of carbon dots presenting as powder, embedded in filter paper and dispersed in solid solution.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Due to larger Stokes shift and longer lifetime, room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) has distinctive advantages over the fluorescence counterparts. RTP materials affording special visualized performance with delayed luminescence at normal temperature are of great interest in applications such as anti-counterfeiting [1,2], chemical sensor [3], imaging [4–6], light-emitting devices [7,8], photocatalysis [9,10] and so forth. However, it is of great challenge to achieve RTP materials due to the spin forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions which are responsible for low probability of intersystem crossing (ISC) and can be much more easily consumed by vibrational deactivation and oxygen quenching at room and higher temperatures [3]. In order to overcome the above factors, enhancing spin-orbit coupling by the introduction of heavy atoms is generally performed to promote ISC efficiency [11–13], and embedding a luminescent species in appropriate solid matrices is always applied to restrict vibration and rotation [14–17]. To date, most of the available phosphorescent materials are still limited to organometallic complexes and metal-free pure organic compounds [18–21]. However, many of these materials suffer from high cost, cytotoxicity, complicated preparation process and sensitivity to oxygen [20,21]. Moreover, short emission lifetimes (i.e., microseconds to a few milliseconds) and prompt decay hamper their applications in some specific fields because tens of milliseconds lifetime is generally required for naked eyes to detect a phosphorescence emission [22]. Therefore, it is still highly desirable to develop new types of RTP materials with effective cost, facile preparation, low cytotoxicity, free noble metal and long lifetime.

As a new class of optical carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in chemosensing [23,24], bioimaging [25], photoelectric device [26,27] and photocatalysis [28,29] and so on [30,31], due to their bright fluorescence [32], simple preparation procedures [33], effective cost [34], excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [35]. Although a few of CDs had been reported to emit RTP, most of them only generated RTP as were embedded in some matrices, including polyvinyl alcohol [36], polyurethane [37], potassium aluminium sulfate [15,37], recrystallized urea/biuret [38] and layered double hydroxides [39], which are crucial for stabilizing the long lifetime triplet states. Recently, CDs directly emitting RTP without being fixed in matrices have been increasingly obtained, occurring as a result of one of the following causes: 1) interior hydrogen bonds formed between the functional groups of CDs, thus to stabilize excited triplet states [40]; 2) New energy levels created by doping N and P elements, and n-π * electron transition between singlet and triplet states with a small energy gap [41]; 3) Hydrogen and covalent interactions between guests and hosts to fix and rigidify triplet emission species [42]. Thereafter, long-lived RTP CDs with high performances will be more possible to be achieved by facile methods with the guide of these principles.

In this work, long-lived RTP carbon dots were synthesized by a facile and prompt microwave-assisted pyrolysis of ethylenediamine (EDA) in aqueous solution of phosphoric acid (EDA-CDs). The fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder were analyzed in detail. Interestingly, at room temperature, EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder can emit bright phosphorescence, lasting for 7 s to naked eyes, with the lifetime being up to 693.67 and 887.55 ms, respectively(see Scheme 1 ). However, the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs in solid solution can reach 1538 ms at 123 K which is much longer than 1287.54 ms of solid powder at the same temperature. Finally, the RTP EDA-CDs had shown a great potential to be as advanced security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

EDA (≥99.0%), citric acid (CA) (≥99.0%), oxalic acid (OA) (≥99.0%), ethylene glycol (≥99%) and HCl (36–38%) were obtained from Aladin Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid (85%) and sulphuric acid (98%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals used in this work are of analytical reagents without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3MΩ.cm).

The morphology and size of EDA-CDs were analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7000, operated at 80 kV). UV–vis absorption spectra were performed by the use of a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescent spectra were collected by a fluorescence spectrometer F-4600 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of EDA-CDs was recorded using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40 kV, 40 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 10° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the EDA-CDs was scanned using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with KBr pellets technique, over the range 500–4000 cm−1.

EDA-CDs was prepared according to a microwave method [43,44]. Firstly, 16 mL deionized water and 14.3% (V/V) EDA were mixed well, and 8 mL H3PO4 were put into conical flask under vigorous stirring, and then the beaker was placed into a microwave oven and heated for 3.5 min at 700 W. After the product was cooled down to room temperature, another 40 mL purified water was added and sonicated for 15 min. The resultant mixture was centrifuged (10000 r min−1) to remove any large or agglomerated particles, and then the supernatant were dialyzed against purified water using a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 72 h. Finally, the amorphous solid powder of EDA-CDs was collected by lyophilization.

Control experiments were performed by using ethylene glycol and oxalic acid to replace EDA, and using sulphuric acid to replace phosphoric acid under the same reaction conditions for the CDs syntheses. The products obtained from ethylene glycol, oxalic acid and sulphuric acid were named as EG-CDs, EA-CDs and S-CDs, respectively.

QYs of the obtained EDA-CDs were determined by a relative method [45,46]. Specially, quinine (QY = 56% in ethanol) was chosen for the determination of quantum yield. The QY of a sample was then calculated according to the following equation:
φ =
φ
′
×
A
I
′
′
×
I
A
×
n
2
n
′
2
where φ is the QY of the testing sample, I is the testing sample's integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.36 for 0.1 M H2SO4), and A is the optical density. The superscript “′” refers to the referenced fluorescence dyes of known QYs (refractive index, n, had also to be considered).
Absolute quantum yield is achieved at the emission wavelength of 410 nm by Edinburg FLS 1000 under excitation at 340 nm.
EDA-CDs aqueous dispersion (300 mg mL−1) can be directly used as security printing ink for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of an interference fluorescent CA-CDs and inks
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the interference fluorescenceCDs, 1 g citric acid and 1.2 mL EDA were added into 5 mL water and reacted under microwave 700 W for 9 min. After cooling to room temperature, 10 mL deionized water was added and sonicated for 10 min to obtain yellow CDs solution. The macromolecules and similar particles were removed by 0.22 μm filter membrane. Some small molecules were removed from the supernatant by dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da. And then, the filtrate liquid was evaporated and concentrated by rotating evaporation. Finally, the CA-CDs solid powders was obtained by freeze drying.

The resultant blue fluorescence CA-CDs were dispersed in water (5 mg mL−1) and employed as one kind of interference fluorescent ink for information encryption. Another kind of interference ink is fluorescein at the concentration of 1 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, a very simple and prompt method to prepare long lived RTP CDs was developed by microwave assisted pyrolysis of EDA in phosphoric acid solution. The optimal synthesis conditions were obtained by optimizing the reaction parameters, including microwave power, EDA concentration and reaction time, to pursue the highest relative fluorescent quantum yield of CDs (quinine sulfate as the reference: 54.6%). Firstly, the effect of microwave power on the product was investigated by measuring the relative quantum yields of the as-obtained CDs under different microwave powers (see Fig. S1). The QY of the as-obtained CDs increased with microwave power rising and reached the maximum at 100% power output (700 W). Under the optimal power, the concentration of EDA was then optimized according to the QYs of the CDs synthesized at different concentrations of EDA. Although QY could reach the highest (16.5%) upon addition of EDA at the volume ratio of 20.0% (VEDA/Vtotal), it is very dangerous because the solution was bumping after addition of such quantity of EDA. Therefore, 14.3% EDA was finally selected as the optimal quantity of EDA. For reaction time, the QYs were not significantly different between 4.5 min and 3.5 min, but the production yield arrived at the maximum at the reaction time of 3.5 min. Ultimately, 700 W, 4 mL EDA and 3.5 min were selected as the optimal conditions to synthesize the CDs (EDA-CDs) for characterization and application.

The morphology and size of EDA-CDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, EDA-CDs exhibit well-dispersed quasi-spherical shapes with an average size of about 13.2 ± 1.4 nm (based on statistical analysis of 100 dots). Two peaks were observed in the XRD profile of EDA-CDs, one is at 2θ = 25.6° with the interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm corresponding to the graphitic carbon based materials [47], while the other is at 2θ = 13.8° being consistent with the amorphous carbon based materials [48] (Fig. 2 c).

FTIR was used to identify the functional groups on the surface of EDA-CDs (Fig. 2d). The broad vibration band of EDA-CDs from about 3600 to 2450 cm−1 is ascribed to –OH, –NH2, =CH and CH stretching vibrations. The absorption bands at 1633 and 1539 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of CN and the bending vibration of N–H. The peaks at 1450, 1231, 1070, and 1007 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration of C–H, the stretching vibrations of PO/C–N, C–O/P–O, and P–N, respectively [1,49].

The photophysical properties of EDA-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution were fully investigated by collecting its absorption, excitation and emission spectra. As can be seen from Fig.2a and Fig. S2, EDA-CDs in aqueous solution exhibit typical excitation-dependent fluorescence feature with the maximum emission at 410 nm under excitation at 340 nm. Its aqueous solution presented canary yellow under sunlight and blue color fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation at 365 nm, respectively (Insert in Fig. 2a). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 2b, there are two major absorption peaks at 280 and 340 nm in the absorption spectrum of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, attributing to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CC and CN, respectively. Notably, the excitation spectrum is almost the same as the absorption spectrum (Fig. 2b and c), implying that non-radiative transition scarcely occurred in EDA-CDs [50]. Meanwhile, the absolute QY of EDA-CDs was measured to be 14.01% by FLS-1000 (Fig. S3).

Differing from typical CDs with aggregation induced fluorescence quenching, EDA-CDs embedded in drying filter paper or presenting as solid powder can both emit bright fluorescence (Figs. S4a and c). Embedding EDA-CDs in drying filter paper was performed by soaking a piece of filter paper with aqueous solution at the concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 and subsequent drying. Compared to EDA-CDs in the soaking aqueous solution, the fluorescence emission of EDA-CDs embedded in the filter paper became less dependent on excitation wavelength, while the emission of EDA-CDs powder was totally independent on excitation wavelength. It seems that aggregation made the fluorescent centers of EDA-CDs more uniform. The maximum emission wavelengths of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder are 410, 415 and 455 nm, indicating that aggregation induced its maximum emission wavelength redshifts (Fig. 2c). Moreover, full width at half maxima (FWHM) of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in the filter paper and presenting as solid powder are 80, 97 and 150 nm, implying that aggregation can also broaden FWHM of EDA-CDs. Additionally, emission redshift, excitation independence and FWHMs broadening of EDA-CDs induced by aggregation might result from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between multiple emitters [51,52].

More interestingly, EDA-CDs embedded in the drying filter paper or presenting as solid powder can both emit green phosphorescence. As can be seen from the video (S1 and S2), blue fluorescence was emitted when EDA-CDs embedded in the drying filter paper or presenting as solid powder excited by UV light at 365 nm, after the UV light turned off, green phosphorescence detected by naked eyes could last for 7 s. As shown in Fig.2d and Fig. S4c and d, EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder exhibit very similar excitation spectrum and excitation independent phosphorescence emission spectrum. Just the RTP emission wavelength of EDA-CDs was red-shifted from 502 nm for embedded in the filter paper to 510 nm for solid powder (see Fig. 2d). Therefore, aggregation of EDA-CDs also induced phosphorescence redshift, which may also result from energy transfer between multiple phosphorescence emitters [53].
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.116851

The following are the supplementary data related to this article:
Video S1 Video S1
Video S2 Video S2

In order to better understand its optical properties, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder were investigated at room temperature (see Figs. S5a and b). The fluorescence lifetimes of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder are 4.87, 4.98, 6.76 ns (Table S1). And the phosphorescence lifetimes of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder are 693.67 and 887.5 ms (Table S2), indicating that EDA-CDs is a kind of excellent RTP materials. Moreover, with EDA-CDs being more aggregated from embedded in filter paper to solid powder, its fluorescence lifetime and phosphorescence lifetimes became longer. Furthermore, the phosphorescence spectra of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper by soaking with aqueous solutions at different concentrations were collected. As can be seen from Fig. S6, the maximum phosphorescence emission wavelength of EDA-CDs is shifted from 500 nm with the concentration of EDA-CDs in soaking solution increasing from 1 to 200 mg mL−1, till to 510 nm of solid powder, which further suggests that aggregation induce phosphorescence emission redshift due to FRET [54].

To further explore the optical properties of EDA-CDs, the effect of temperature on the phosphorescence of its powder was subsequently evaluated. As shown in Fig. S7, the phosphorescence intensity decreased with temperature increasing from 77 to 295 K, suggesting that phosphorescence profited from low temperature. On the other hand, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of EDA-CDs powder at different temperature were measured. As temperature decreased, the fluorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs powder gradually increased, and reached the highest value of 8.59 ns at 123 K (see Fig. 3 a and Table S3). Meanwhile, lower temperature was, longer phosphorescence lifetime exhibited (Fig. 3b). At 77 K, the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs powder could reach 1287.54 ms, while it sharply decreased to 212.42 ms at the highest temperature of 320 K (Table S4). Therefore, low temperature is beneficial to the luminescence of EDA-CDs, resulting from that it enhanced the rigidity of EDA-CDs to avoid the collision quenching and stabilize the triplet excitons for phosphorescence emission. On the other hand, the results from the effect of temperature on the phosphorescence proved that the green afterglow originated from phosphorescence rather than thermal activated delayed fluorescence [55,56].

The photostability of EDA-CDs in aqueous solution, embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder were explored by measuring their peak intensities within 10 min irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3c, EDA-CDs in aqueous solution shows considerable photostability and its fluorescence intensity can remain 98% after 10 min irradiation. However, EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder exhibit poor fluorescence photostability, their fluorescence intensities decreased to about 53% and 52% after 10 min irradiation, respectively. On the other hand, the phosphorescence stabilities of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder were also investigated by measuring their phosphorescence peak intensities within 10 min irradiation. As shown in Fig. 3d, the phosphorescence intensities of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder decreased to 66% and 80%, respectively. Easier absorption of water for filter paper caused EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper quenching more than powder within 10 min irradiation. From the discussion above, it seems that aggregation can decrease the photostability of EDA-CDs, which may result from that aggregation is helpful for producing heat within irradiation, and temperature enhancement can decrease their luminescence intensity.

No phosphorescence could be detected when EDA-CDs was dispersed in aqueous solution at room temperature, even the solution became solid at 223 K. However, as shown in Fig. S7, when the temperature decreased to 173 K, strong phosphorescence was generated by the solid solution, and its average lifetime is so long as 1189 ms (Table S6). Furthermore, the longest average lifetime of EDA-CDs solid solution is 1538 ms at 123 K, which is much longer than that of solid powder at 77 K. Fig. S8 is the photographs of EDA-CDs solid solution taken at 77 K under daylight, light irradiation at 340 nm and after ceasing the irradiation.

The luminescence mechanism of EDA-CDs was proposed as schematically illustrated in Scheme 2 . Since there are several luminescence centers from abundant functional groups, such as –OH, –NH2, CN, C–N, C–O, N–P and PO, on the surface of EDA-CDs, its emission exhibited excitation dependent when dispersed in aqueous solution [57,58]. After aggregated on filter paper or as solid powder, the distance between particles became short enough for FRET, which resulted in that the high energy emission of one dot could be reabsorbed as excitation for another particle [59]. Finally, emissions at low wavelengths were converted to ones at long wavelength. Therefore, the emissions of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presented as solid powder were observed to be red-shifted and excitation independent compared to its aqueous solution. Moreover, aggregation of EDA-CDs in filter paper or as solid powder led to non-radiative cross relaxation between the dots, thus to lower fluorescence intensities and longer lifetimes compared to good dispersion [59,60].

In aqueous solution, a layer of highly ordered hydration water molecules were strongly absorbed on the surface of EDA-CDs by hydrogen bonds formed with the surface functional groups [17]. At room temperature, the collisions from particles and water molecules, as well as dissolved oxygen could quench the phosphorescence of EDA-CDs. Aggregated in filter paper or as solid powder, the functional groups on the surface of EDA-CDs could form hydrogen bonds between particles (interparticle hydrogen bonds) or groups on one particle (intraparticle hydrogen bonds), which could substantially stabilize the triple states and restrict penetration of oxygen for RTP [40]. And, with temperature decreasing, the lifetime EDA-CDs as solid powder increased as a result of more rigidified hydrogen bonds. On the other hand, when the aqueous solution became icy at extremely low temperature, highly ordered hydration water molecules absorbed on the surface of EDA-CDs constructed robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks between two different dots of EDA-CDs as well as the dots with frozen water bulk, which not only effectively rigidified the functional groups on EDA-CDs and subsequently enhanced the rigidity of the entire system, but also froze the motion of dissolved O2 [17], resulting in the enhancement of phosphorescence and longer phosphorescence lifetime than those of solid powder (Fig. S8).

In order to further understand the effects of N and P doping on RTP emission of EDA-CDs, CDs were synthesized by ethylene glycol and oxalic acid substituting for EDA (EG-CDs and EA-CDs), and sulphuric acid replacing phosphoric acid (S-CDs), under the same conditions. The fluorescence spectra of these CDs without N or P doping are similar to these of EDA-CDs, which are excitation–dependent, and the emission peaks are all centered at about 410 nm (Fig. S9). Nevertheless, they could not emit RTP (see Fig. S9d). Therefore, N and P co-doping would facilitate the ISC for effectively populating triplet excitons.

Due to its unique fluorescence and phosphorescence performances, EDA-CDs has a great potential to be as advanced security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. Therefore, encryption/decoding tests using EDA-CDs as security ink were carried out by pouring the solutions of EDA-

CDs&CA-CDs, fluorescein and CA-CDs into red, yellow and blue part of an ink box, respectively. Then the designed patterns were printed on commercial filter paper without auto-fluorescence by using a desktop inkjet printer (HP Deskjet 2029 printer, see Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 4 a, a microarray data storage pattern according to the binary codes of the standard 8 bit ASCII characters, with red and blue representing ‘1’ and ‘0’, respectively. After printed on filter paper, all square block showed blue fluorescence under UV excitation at 365 nm, called ‘goose code’. As the UV light was switched off, EDA-CDs continuous emitted delayed phosphorescence emission, and the ‘real code’ can be easily read and translated into ‘01010001’, ‘01000001’ and ‘01010101’, corresponding to capital letters ‘Q’, ‘A’ and ’U’ according to ASCII binary code. Also, information was encrypted and transmitted using the Chinese traditional telegraph code combined with the RTP properties of EDA-CDs, in which the password information was printed using EDA-CDs solution, the interference information was characterized by blue color (see Fig. 4b). Under the UV light, ‘8888 8888 8888’ in blue color was exhibited as a goose code. After the UV light switched off, the patterned security code of ‘7230 3593 0714’ was shown as bright green phosphorescence, which can be decoded as the Chinese word ‘qing’, ‘nong’, ‘da’.

According to the phosphorescence properties of EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper, it was also applied for word and graphic anti-counterfeiting. A serious of words ‘CARBON DOTS carbon dots … ’ were printed on filter paper, under ultraviolet light all text displayed blue fluorescence, while EDA-CDs marked words emitted green delayed light (Fig. 4c). For graphic anti-counterfeiting, as shown in Fig. S11, a graph containing the badge with Chinese and English names of our university was printed on a piece of filter paper, it was displayed as a blurred image under visible and UV light and as a clear image after ultraviolet light irradiation turned off.

In this work, long-lived RTP carbon dots can be synthesized by a facile and prompt microwave-assisted pyrolysis of EDA in aqueous solution of phosphoric acid. EDA-CDs embedded in filter paper and presenting as solid powder emit bright phosphorescence at room temperature. With the help of robust bridge-like hydrogen-bonded networks formed by highly ordered hydration water molecules around EDA-CDs in solid solution at low temperature, the phosphorescence lifetime of EDA-CDs in solid solution is much longer than that of solid powder. Finally, the RTP EDA-CDs had shown a great potential to be as advanced security ink for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0105200), Key Research and Development Project of Shandong Province (No. 2018GNC110006, 2016GSF116007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.116851.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, Y. Wang, C. Cai, H. Lin, Conversion of Carbon Dots from Fluorescence to Ultralong Room-Temperature Phosphorescence by Heating for Security Applications, Advanced materials, 30 (2018) 1800783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Triple-mode emission of carbon dots: applications for advanced anti-counterfeiting
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, L. Zhang, J. Lu, C. Xu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Triple-Mode Emission of Carbon Dots: Applications for Advanced Anti-Counterfeiting, Angewandte Chemie International Edition, 55 (2016) 7231-7235.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: New red-emitting schiff base chelates: promising dyes for sensing and imaging of temperature and oxygen via phosphorescence decay time
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.M. Borisov, R. Pommer, J. Svec, S. Peters, I. Klimant, New Red-Emitting Schiff Base Chelates: Promising Dyes for Sensing and Imaging of Temperature and Oxygen via Phosphorescence Decay Time, Journal of Materials Chemistry C, 6 (2018) 8999-9009.
